original_abstract: The dual roles of minerals in inhibiting and prolonging extracellular enzyme activity in soils and sediments are governed by enzyme adsorption to mineral surfaces. Oxygenation of mineral-bound Fe(II) generates reactive oxygen species (ROS), yet it is unknown whether and how this process alters the activity and functional lifespan of extracellular enzymes. Here, the effect of mineral-bound Fe(II) oxidation on the hydrolytic activity of a cellulose-degrading enzyme β-glucosidase (BG) was studied using two pre-reduced Fe-bearing clay minerals (nontronite and montmorillonite) and one pre-reduced iron oxide (magnetite) at pH 5 and 7. Under anoxic conditions, BG adsorption to mineral surfaces decreased its activity but prolonged its lifespan. Under oxic conditions, ROS was produced, with the amount of •OH, the most abundant ROS, being positively correlated with the extent of structural Fe(II) oxidation in reduced minerals. •OH decreased BG activity and shortened its lifespan via conformational change and structural decomposition of BG. These results suggest that under oxic conditions, the ROS-induced inhibitory role of Fe(II)-bearing minerals outweighed their adsorption-induced protective role in controlling enzyme activity. These results disclose a previously unknown mechanism of extracellular enzyme inactivation, which have pivotal implications for predicting the active enzyme pool in redox-oscillating environments.
humanized_abstract: Here we investigate for the first time how the oxidation of mineral-bound Fe(II) affects the hydrolytic activity of a cellulose-degrading enzyme, the enzyme β-glucosidase (BG), using two pre-reduced Fe-bearing clay minerals and one pre-reduced iron oxide. Experiments were carried out at pH 5 and 7, under both anoxic and oxic conditions. In the absence of ROS (i.e., anoxic conditions), enzyme adsorption to mineral surfaces was associated with both lower initial activities and longer-lasting activity, possibly reflecting a trade-off between catalytic performance and enzyme stability. Under aerobic conditions, ROS such as hydroxyl radicals (•OH) were detected, with their amounts highly correlated with the degree of structural Fe(II) oxidation in the reduced minerals. These ROS significantly impaired enzyme catalysis. This enzymatic activity loss was attributed to a combined effects of ROS-induced protein unfolding and enzyme structural degradation. Therefore, under aerobic conditions, the detrimental effects of ROS on enzyme catalysis related to Fe(II)-bearing minerals clearly dominated the protective role of the enzyme adsorption to minerals surfaces. Our work thereby identifies for the first time a new enzymatic activity-inactivating mechanism under aerobic redox environment with implications to the estimation of the bioavailable enzyme activity in the dynamic soil and sediment environments.
